title: ADHD in Adults
oneLiner: Neurodevelopmental disorder with persistent inattention/hyperactivity-impulsivity affecting ~3–4% adults; untreated increases risk of occupational failure, substance misuse, and accidents.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Hypertensive crisis / catecholamine surge** (from stimulant overdose or interaction with MAOI): BP >180/120, headache, chest pain, palpitations — *single finding: systolic BP >200 or HR >140 with neuro signs* (seizure, stroke).
- **Cardiac arrhythmia (VT/VF)** in undiagnosed structural heart disease (e.g. HCM, prolonged QT) after starting stimulant: syncope, family history of sudden cardiac death — *single finding: ECG shows LVH, pathological Q waves, or QTc >500 ms*.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms** (DSM-5 criteria adapted for adults):
  - Inattention: difficulty sustaining focus, careless mistakes, poor time management, losing things.
  - Hyperactivity: inner restlessness, fidgeting, talking excessively, difficulty relaxing.
  - Impulsivity: interrupting, acting without thinking, poor decision-making, road rage.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Normal physical exam (but check BP, HR, BMI, thyroid).
  - May appear fidgety, talk fast, unable to sit still.
  - Look for excoriations (picking skin), signs of substance use (pupils, track marks).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Anxiety disorders** (GAD, panic) – worry-focused, autonomic arousal, but no childhood onset of inattention.
- **Bipolar disorder** (especially in hypomania) – episodic, grandiosity, decreased need for sleep.
- **Depression** – low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor slowing (vs hyperactivity).
- **Sleep disorders** (sleep apnoea, insufficient sleep syndrome) – daytime drowsiness, snoring, obesity.
- **Thyroid dysfunction** – weight changes, heat/cold intolerance, goitre.
- **Substance misuse** (cocaine, amphetamines) – acute intoxication, withdrawal, or chronic use pattern.
- **Splitter:** Childhood-onset (before age 12) of inattention/hyperactivity = favours ADHD; adult-onset = more likely anxiety/depression/substance.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (baseline — mandatory before stimulant therapy).
  - BP, HR, weight, height, BMI.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, TFTs (to rule out medical causes).
  - Drug screen if substance misuse suspected.
- **Imaging:** None routine.
- **Special tests:**
  - DIVA-5 (Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults) or ASRS (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale) – screening/criteria.
  - Collateral history from parent/partner/teacher (essential for childhood onset).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** DSM-5 or ICD-10/11 — requires ≥5 symptoms in either or both domains (inattention, hyperactivity-impulsivity) present for ≥6 months, onset before age 12, across multiple settings, with functional impairment.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** before prescribing any stimulant — to rule out long QT, LVH, or conduction defects (sudden cardiac death risk).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute stimulant toxicity: supportive care, benzodiazepines for agitation, monitor BP/HR/ECG.
  - If new diagnosis suspected in crisis (anxiety/depression): treat acute distress first; do not start ADHD meds until stable.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - For patients admitted on ADHD meds: continue regular doses if possible (avoid abrupt withdrawal — can precipitate depression/agitation). Check with psychiatric liaison.
  - Monitor BP/HR and ECG.
  - Assess for side effects: appetite, sleep, mood changes.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to specialist ADHD clinic (psychiatry or specialist nurse).
  - Psychological intervention: psychoeducation, CBT for adults with ADHD (NICE recommends group or individual).
  - Pharmacotherapy: initiated by specialist; shared care with GP after stabilisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: no specific requirement, but encourage oral intake (stimulants can suppress appetite).
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard unless contraindicated.
  - Diet/NBM: nil specific; monitor weight.
  - Observations: routine, plus BP/HR pre-dose and 1-hour post-dose if newly started.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Methylphenidate* (e.g. Concerta XL, Equasym XL, Medikinet XL): immediate- and modified-release; dose titration [check local policy] — typically start 5–10 mg MR once daily, increase by 5–10 mg weekly, max 60–100 mg/day depending on preparation.
  - *Lisdexamfetamine* (Elvanse): prodrug, once daily; start 20–30 mg, increase by 10–20 mg weekly, max 70 mg/day.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *Dexamfetamine* (Dexedrine): immediate-release; dose [check local policy] typically 5–40 mg/day in divided doses.
  - *Atomoxetine* (Strattera): non-stimulant, selective noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor; start 40 mg for 7 days then increase to 80 mg; max 100 mg/day.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - MAOIs (hypertensive crisis); avoid within 14 days.
  - Cardiovascular disease (severe HTN, arrhythmia, ischaemic heart disease).
  - Pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism, glaucoma.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Avoid atomoxetine in severe hepatic impairment (metabolised by CYP2D6; dose reduction in moderate impairment). Methylphenidate and lisdexamfetamine – caution in severe renal impairment; [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting a stimulant without ECG and BP check** — missed cardiac risk = sudden death. Also: combining stimulants with MAOIs.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Hypertension, tachycardia, palpitations.
  - Insomnia, appetite suppression, weight loss.
  - Agitation, psychosis (rare, at high doses or misuse).
- **Chronic:**
  - Cardiovascular strain (chronic HTN, increased heart rate).
  - Growth delay in children/adolescents; less relevant in adults.
  - Dependence and misuse potential (especially immediate-release stimulants).
  - Poor self-esteem, occupational underachievement.
sections.Prognosis: - ADHD persists into adulthood in ~60% of childhood cases. With appropriate treatment (medication + psychological support), most achieve significant symptom reduction and functional improvement. Untreated: higher rates of unemployment, substance misuse, accidents, and relationship difficulties.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always take a collateral history** (parent/partner) — childhood onset is essential for diagnosis.
- **Perform ECG before starting any stimulant** and repeat after dose stabilisation to check QTc.
- **Start low, go slow** — titrate methylphenidate/lisdexamfetamine by 5–10 mg weekly to minimise side effects.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not diagnose ADHD solely on self-report** in the ED — many conditions cause similar symptoms; always wait for specialist assessment.
- **Do not stop stimulants abruptly** in a stable patient admitted for unrelated reason — withdrawal can cause severe depression, fatigue, and suicidal ideation. Liaise with psychiatric team.
- **Do not prescribe stimulants to patients with acute psychosis/mania** without psychiatric input — can worsen symptoms and trigger relapse.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Chest pain, palpitations, syncope, severe headache or visual changes (hypertensive crisis), new-onset suicidal thoughts, severe agitation or mania, signs of psychosis (hallucinations, paranoia).
- **Follow-up:** Referral to adult ADHD specialist service; if already on medication, ensure regular monitoring (3–6 monthly for BP, HR, weight) as per shared care protocol.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call senior/psychiatric liaison if: **NEWS2 ≥5** (any cause), GCS <15, new-onset chest pain or syncope, BP >180/110 despite withholding medication, suicidal ideation with intent, or acute psychosis.
- ICU referral for stimulant overdose with haemodynamic instability, seizures, or hyperthermia.

rawMarkdown:
## ADHD in Adults
**One-liner:** Neurodevelopmental disorder with persistent inattention/hyperactivity-impulsivity affecting ~3–4% adults; untreated increases risk of occupational failure, substance misuse, and accidents.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Hypertensive crisis / catecholamine surge** (from stimulant overdose or interaction with MAOI): BP >180/120, headache, chest pain, palpitations — *single finding: systolic BP >200 or HR >140 with neuro signs* (seizure, stroke).
- **Cardiac arrhythmia (VT/VF)** in undiagnosed structural heart disease (e.g. HCM, prolonged QT) after starting stimulant: syncope, family history of sudden cardiac death — *single finding: ECG shows LVH, pathological Q waves, or QTc >500 ms*.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms** (DSM-5 criteria adapted for adults):
  - Inattention: difficulty sustaining focus, careless mistakes, poor time management, losing things.
  - Hyperactivity: inner restlessness, fidgeting, talking excessively, difficulty relaxing.
  - Impulsivity: interrupting, acting without thinking, poor decision-making, road rage.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Normal physical exam (but check BP, HR, BMI, thyroid).
  - May appear fidgety, talk fast, unable to sit still.
  - Look for excoriations (picking skin), signs of substance use (pupils, track marks).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Anxiety disorders** (GAD, panic) – worry-focused, autonomic arousal, but no childhood onset of inattention.
- **Bipolar disorder** (especially in hypomania) – episodic, grandiosity, decreased need for sleep.
- **Depression** – low mood, anhedonia, psychomotor slowing (vs hyperactivity).
- **Sleep disorders** (sleep apnoea, insufficient sleep syndrome) – daytime drowsiness, snoring, obesity.
- **Thyroid dysfunction** – weight changes, heat/cold intolerance, goitre.
- **Substance misuse** (cocaine, amphetamines) – acute intoxication, withdrawal, or chronic use pattern.
- **Splitter:** Childhood-onset (before age 12) of inattention/hyperactivity = favours ADHD; adult-onset = more likely anxiety/depression/substance.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG (baseline — mandatory before stimulant therapy).
  - BP, HR, weight, height, BMI.
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&Es, LFTs, TFTs (to rule out medical causes).
  - Drug screen if substance misuse suspected.
- **Imaging:** None routine.
- **Special tests:**
  - DIVA-5 (Diagnostic Interview for ADHD in Adults) or ASRS (Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale) – screening/criteria.
  - Collateral history from parent/partner/teacher (essential for childhood onset).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** DSM-5 or ICD-10/11 — requires ≥5 symptoms in either or both domains (inattention, hyperactivity-impulsivity) present for ≥6 months, onset before age 12, across multiple settings, with functional impairment.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** before prescribing any stimulant — to rule out long QT, LVH, or conduction defects (sudden cardiac death risk).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If acute stimulant toxicity: supportive care, benzodiazepines for agitation, monitor BP/HR/ECG.
  - If new diagnosis suspected in crisis (anxiety/depression): treat acute distress first; do not start ADHD meds until stable.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - For patients admitted on ADHD meds: continue regular doses if possible (avoid abrupt withdrawal — can precipitate depression/agitation). Check with psychiatric liaison.
  - Monitor BP/HR and ECG.
  - Assess for side effects: appetite, sleep, mood changes.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Refer to specialist ADHD clinic (psychiatry or specialist nurse).
  - Psychological intervention: psychoeducation, CBT for adults with ADHD (NICE recommends group or individual).
  - Pharmacotherapy: initiated by specialist; shared care with GP after stabilisation.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - Fluids: no specific requirement, but encourage oral intake (stimulants can suppress appetite).
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard unless contraindicated.
  - Diet/NBM: nil specific; monitor weight.
  - Observations: routine, plus BP/HR pre-dose and 1-hour post-dose if newly started.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - *Methylphenidate* (e.g. Concerta XL, Equasym XL, Medikinet XL): immediate- and modified-release; dose titration [check local policy] — typically start 5–10 mg MR once daily, increase by 5–10 mg weekly, max 60–100 mg/day depending on preparation.
  - *Lisdexamfetamine* (Elvanse): prodrug, once daily; start 20–30 mg, increase by 10–20 mg weekly, max 70 mg/day.
- **Common alternatives:**
  - *Dexamfetamine* (Dexedrine): immediate-release; dose [check local policy] typically 5–40 mg/day in divided doses.
  - *Atomoxetine* (Strattera): non-stimulant, selective noradrenaline reuptake inhibitor; start 40 mg for 7 days then increase to 80 mg; max 100 mg/day.
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - MAOIs (hypertensive crisis); avoid within 14 days.
  - Cardiovascular disease (severe HTN, arrhythmia, ischaemic heart disease).
  - Pheochromocytoma, hyperthyroidism, glaucoma.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Avoid atomoxetine in severe hepatic impairment (metabolised by CYP2D6; dose reduction in moderate impairment). Methylphenidate and lisdexamfetamine – caution in severe renal impairment; [check local policy].
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting a stimulant without ECG and BP check** — missed cardiac risk = sudden death. Also: combining stimulants with MAOIs.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Hypertension, tachycardia, palpitations.
  - Insomnia, appetite suppression, weight loss.
  - Agitation, psychosis (rare, at high doses or misuse).
- **Chronic:**
  - Cardiovascular strain (chronic HTN, increased heart rate).
  - Growth delay in children/adolescents; less relevant in adults.
  - Dependence and misuse potential (especially immediate-release stimulants).
  - Poor self-esteem, occupational underachievement.

### Prognosis
- ADHD persists into adulthood in ~60% of childhood cases. With appropriate treatment (medication + psychological support), most achieve significant symptom reduction and functional improvement. Untreated: higher rates of unemployment, substance misuse, accidents, and relationship difficulties.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always take a collateral history** (parent/partner) — childhood onset is essential for diagnosis.
- **Perform ECG before starting any stimulant** and repeat after dose stabilisation to check QTc.
- **Start low, go slow** — titrate methylphenidate/lisdexamfetamine by 5–10 mg weekly to minimise side effects.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not diagnose ADHD solely on self-report** in the ED — many conditions cause similar symptoms; always wait for specialist assessment.
- **Do not stop stimulants abruptly** in a stable patient admitted for unrelated reason — withdrawal can cause severe depression, fatigue, and suicidal ideation. Liaise with psychiatric team.
- **Do not prescribe stimulants to patients with acute psychosis/mania** without psychiatric input — can worsen symptoms and trigger relapse.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Chest pain, palpitations, syncope, severe headache or visual changes (hypertensive crisis), new-onset suicidal thoughts, severe agitation or mania, signs of psychosis (hallucinations, paranoia).
- **Follow-up:** Referral to adult ADHD specialist service; if already on medication, ensure regular monitoring (3–6 monthly for BP, HR, weight) as per shared care protocol.

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior/psychiatric liaison if: **NEWS2 ≥5** (any cause), GCS <15, new-onset chest pain or syncope, BP >180/110 despite withholding medication, suicidal ideation with intent, or acute psychosis.
- ICU referral for stimulant overdose with haemodynamic instability, seizures, or hyperthermia.
